sourceTitle: Break tasks into manageable steps with sub-tasks and dependencies

questions.question: What Notion feature is used to build sub-tasks and dependencies?

options.explanation: Formulas calculate values; they don't create links between items.
Relation properties connect items in a database, enabling sub‑tasks and dependencies.
Rollups display aggregated data from relations but don't establish the relationship itself.
Template buttons automate content creation; they don't define task relationships.

questions.question: Which menu option do you select first to create a sub‑item property in a tasks database?

options.explanation: While you can add a property here, the guide specifies using the … menu, not the generic properties pane.
The guide instructs to click the … menu in the top‑right of the database and choose Sub‑items.
Timeline view is for visualizing dependencies, not for creating sub‑item properties.
Filters help narrow results; they don't create new properties.

questions.question: When you add a dependency in timeline view, what visual cue indicates a dependency between two tasks?

options.text: A colored dot on the task bar
options.explanation: Dots represent status markers, not dependencies.

options.text: An arrow that appears when you hover over the task
options.explanation: Hovering shows an arrow on the right side that you can drag to connect tasks, creating a dependency.

options.text: A checkmark that appears next to the task name
options.explanation: Checkmarks denote completed items, not dependencies.
Background colors are used for grouping, not for showing dependencies.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of using sub‑tasks and dependencies according to the guide?

options.text: They help identify blockers that halt progress
options.explanation: Identifying blockers is explicitly listed as a benefit.

options.text: They automatically assign tasks to the most available team member
options.explanation: The guide does not claim automatic assignment; it only facilitates manual delegation.

options.text: They allow you to break large tasks into smaller, assignable steps
options.explanation: Breaking tasks into smaller steps is a core advantage.

options.text: They make it easier to collaborate across team members
options.explanation: Collaboration is highlighted as a benefit.

questions.question: If you want sub‑tasks to appear as a separate section on a task page, which option should you configure?

options.text: Set the page’s default view to Gallery
options.explanation: Changing the view type does not affect sub‑task placement on the page.

options.text: Choose to show sub‑items as a page section in the page settings
options.explanation: The guide states you can display sub‑items as a page section for immediate visibility.

options.text: Enable the ‘Show as timeline’ toggle in the database properties
options.explanation: Timeline view affects visualization, not the page layout.

options.text: Apply a filter that only shows parent tasks
options.explanation: Filtering changes which rows appear, not how sub‑items are displayed on a page.

questions.question: When creating a self‑relating property for sub‑tasks, what are the default names of the two new properties?

options.explanation: The guide uses “Sub‑tasks” and “Parent tasks,” not “Child tasks.”
These are the default labels shown after creating the sub‑item property.
Those names belong to the dependency relation, not the sub‑task relation.
Only one property would be named “Sub‑tasks”; the guide specifies both names.

questions.question: Which view(s) can display sub‑tasks once they have been added to a parent task?

options.explanation: Sub‑tasks are visible in both Table and Timeline views.
The guide explicitly states sub‑tasks can be viewed in both view types.
Calendar view is not mentioned for sub‑task display.
Gallery view is unrelated to sub‑task visualization per the article.

questions.question: In the context of dependencies, what does the guide mean by “show dependencies by parent or sub‑task”?

options.text: Display the dependent task’s name in the parent’s title field
options.explanation: The setting controls visual grouping, not title text injection.

options.text: Choose whether the arrow points from the parent to the sub‑task or vice‑versa in timeline view
options.explanation: The option determines which side of the relationship is visualized as the source of the dependency.

options.text: Automatically assign the dependent task to the same assignee as the parent
options.explanation: Dependency settings do not affect assignee fields.

options.text: Create a filtered view that only shows parent tasks with dependencies
options.explanation: Filtering is separate from the “show by parent or sub‑task” toggle.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example use case for sub‑items that is NOT mentioned in the guide?

options.explanation: The guide lists this as a suggested use case.

options.text: Creating parent and child teams in an org chart
options.explanation: This example is explicitly described.

options.text: Tracking monthly and weekly updates in a docs database
options.explanation: The guide mentions this scenario.

options.text: Generating automatic reminders for overdue tasks
options.explanation: Automatic reminders are not discussed as a sub‑item application in the article.

questions.question: What is the recommended threshold of rows in a tasks database after which sub‑tasks and dependencies become especially helpful?

options.explanation: The article specifies a larger number.
The guide suggests that once you exceed 50 rows, sub‑tasks and dependencies aid clarity.
While helpful, the article does not set this as the threshold.

options.text: Any number of rows; they’re always necessary
options.explanation: The guide recommends them particularly when the database grows beyond 50 rows.
